title: Union Oyster House turns 200: Inside America’s oldest continuously operating restaurant
author: Nathan Tavares
bullets: Union Oyster House in Boston celebrates its 200th anniversary, being the oldest continuously operating restaurant in the United States.
The restaurant features historic artifacts and rooms, including the Colonial Room and the Kennedy Booth, beloved by John F. Kennedy.
Master chef Americo DiFronzo oversees the kitchen, serving up to 1,500 guests daily, with popular items like clam chowder and oysters.

title: Boston’s biggest summer events collide over the next 8 days. Buckle up.
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Boston will host the Boston Pops Fireworks Spectacular on July 4 featuring Chance the Rapper and a drone show.
A World Cup quarterfinal will take place at Boston Stadium on July 9, causing potential traffic on Route 1.
Sail Boston's Parade of Sail with over 60 tall ships commences on July 11, marking part of America's 250th anniversary celebrations.

title: The presidents, celebrities, and famous regulars of Boston’s Union Oyster House
author: Nathan Tavares
bullets: Union Oyster House, Boston's oldest restaurant, has hosted six U.S. presidents, including JFK and Obama.
Famous guests include celebrities like Matt Damon, Al Pacino, and Meryl Streep over the years.
A 2003 painting at the restaurant caricatures over 50 legendary past visitors at a fictional gathering.

title: N.H. State Police announce unexpected death of 19-year veteran
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Sgt. Brian J. Ross, a 19-year veteran of N.H. State Police, died unexpectedly at age 41.
Ross served in various roles, including the Major Crime Unit and as commander of the Collision Analysis Unit.
Funeral services are scheduled for July 7 in North Hampton; no cause of death was specified.

title: Jaylen Brown says Celtics showed ‘lack of respect’ after trade to Sixers
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Jaylen Brown criticized the Boston Celtics for a lack of respect following his trade to the Philadelphia 76ers.
Brown expressed dissatisfaction with Celtics President Brad Stevens, citing inadequate communication during trade discussions.
Brown was traded for Paul George and draft picks; he did not request the trade and wanted an explanation.

title: Hull man dies after apparent drowning at Nantasket Beach, officials say
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: A 76-year-old Hull man, Robert Nolan, died from an apparent drowning at Nantasket Beach on Wednesday evening, officials report.
The Plymouth County DA confirmed Nolan was unresponsive in the water and pronounced dead at 8:40 p.m.
No foul play is suspected according to authorities, and an examination will be conducted by the Chief Medical Examiner's Office.

title: Don Sweeney, Bruins’ retooling plans remain a work in progress
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Bruins traded goalie Joonas Korpisalo to the Rangers for cap relief, acquiring Will Borgen for future draft picks.
Boston signed Connor Clifton and re-signed Jordan Harris, boosting their defense and maintaining $5.3 million in cap space.
General Manager Don Sweeney indicates possible trades as the Bruins look to manage their crowded roster and cap situation.

title: Parking will be free at state parks, beaches for Fourth of July weekend
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Massachusetts will waive parking fees at all state parks and beaches from July 3-5 for the Fourth of July weekend.
The parking fee waiver marks the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution, as announced by Governor Maura Healey.
Some state beaches may be closed due to water quality issues following sewage leaks; updates available on DCR Park Alerts webpage.

title: What’s up with those online ads for gas-saving gadgets?
author: John Paul
bullets: Gas-saving gadgets like pills, air filters, and magnets lack verified effectiveness, says AAA's Car Doctor, John Paul.
Run-flat tires have improved but still provide a firmer ride and cost more than conventional tires.
John Paul advises timing belt replacement for a 2002 Honda Accord due to age, despite low mileage.

title: Boston civilian board recommends changes to BPD policies on bodycam footage, federal entanglements
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: OPAT recommends Boston Police release bodycam footage within 30 days, consulting families after critical incidents like shootings.
The memo urges Boston Police to restrict cooperation with federal agencies like ICE and DHS, citing civil rights concerns.
OPAT advises against sharing biometric data of non-felony arrestees and suggests oversight for certain policing activities.
